text: This video is demonstrating laparoscopic low anterior resection for a fifty six year old male diagnosed with synchronous sigmoid colon cancer and rectal cancer.
The surgery was performed by a second year fellow in the colorectal division under supervision.
The surgeon begins lateral dissection by detaching the natural adhesions of the sigmoid colon and continues towards the splenic flexure.
Following the completion of lateral dissection, the surgeon separates Toldt's fascia from the mesocolon, revealing the anatomical layers involved in the procedure.
Underneath the Toldt's fascia, the left gonadal vessel and the left ureter are visible, descending within the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes the mesosigmoid by grasping the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, initiating medial dissection from the sacral promontory toward the root of the IMA.

The surgeon creates a peritoneal window between the lateral and medial dissection planes, initiating apical node dissection at the anticipated bifurcation point of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), with the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) visible traversing the bifurcation.

During the dissection of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) nodes, the surgeon carefully navigates around the area to avoid injury to the visible superior hypogastric nerve plexus and preaortic nerve plexus.

Following the completion of the IMA node dissection, the surgeon ligates the IMV and subsequently ligates the supravector artery at the IMA bifurcation, ensuring the preservation of the left colic artery.
The surgeon begins pelvic dissection from the sacral promontory, encountering abundant lusoreolar tissues in the area.
The surgeon dissects the rectal proper fascia from the pre-hypogastric nerve fascia, revealing the underlying hypogastric nerves.
The surgeon uses a tape to hang the mobilized rectum, providing adequate traction for further dissection.
The surgeon makes a U-shaped incision at the peritoneal reflection, revealing the seminal vesicles and the nonvertebral fascia in the surgical field.

During the posterior lateral pelvic dissection, the surgeon carefully navigates around the hypogastric nerves and the inferior hypogastric nerve plexus, which are visible running downward and tethering the rectal proper fascia.
The surgeon incises the rectal sacral fascia, revealing the pelvic floor beneath.

The surgeon performs further anterolateral dissection, carefully avoiding injury to the inferior hypogastric nerve plexus, which is visually identifiable as it continues towards the neurovascular bundles leading to the genitourinary organs.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the rectum, divides the mesorectum at the distal margin, and resects the rectum using linear staplers.
The surgeon uses a Fondation device to create a mini-laparotomy site for specimen extraction, preparing the area for removal of resected tissue.
